stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), so that \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \). The matrix \( B \) is obtained from \( A \) by multiplying every entry of the first row by \( [[k]] \). What is \( \det(B) \)?
solution: Scaling a single row by \( [[k]] \) multiplies the determinant by \( [[k]] \): \( \det(B) = [[k]]\cdot\det(A) = [[k]]\cdot([[detA]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \det(A) \); it forgets that scaling a row scales the determinant.
This scales by \( [[k]]^2 \), the factor for scaling the whole \( 2\times2 \) matrix, not a single row.
This is \( -\det(A) \), the effect of a row swap rather than a row scaling.
This adds \( [[k]] \) to the determinant instead of multiplying by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), so that \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \). The matrix \( C \) is obtained from \( A \) by swapping its two rows. What is \( \det(C) \)?
solution: Swapping two rows negates the determinant: \( \det(C) = -\det(A) = -([[detA]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \det(A) \); it forgets that a row swap flips the sign of the determinant.
This doubles the determinant instead of negating it.
This negates and doubles the determinant at once.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & [[f]] \end{bmatrix} \), an upper-triangular matrix with \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \) (the product of the diagonal entries). Let \( B = [[k]]A \) (every entry of \( A \) multiplied by \( [[k]] \)). What is \( \det(B) \)?
solution: Scaling every entry of an \( n\times n \) matrix by \( [[k]] \) multiplies its determinant by \( [[k]]^n \). Here \( n=3 \), so \( \det(B) = [[k]]^3\det(A) = [[k]]^3\cdot([[detA]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This scales by \( [[k]] \), the factor for scaling only a single row, not the whole \( 3\times3 \) matrix.
This scales by \( [[k]]^2 \), the factor that would apply to a \( 2\times2 \) matrix.
This is \( \det(A) \); it forgets to scale at all.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) and let \( A^{T} \) denote its transpose. What is \( \det(A^{T}) \)?
solution: Transposing a matrix does not change its determinant: \( \det(A^{T}) = \det(A) = ([[a]])([[d]]) - ([[b]])([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This incorrectly assumes transposing negates the determinant, like a row swap does.
This is also \( -\det(A) \), computed a different way.
This multiplies down the wrong pairs of entries entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \) with \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \), and \( B = \begin{bmatrix} [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] \end{bmatrix} \) with \( \det(B) = [[detB]] \). Using the multiplicative property of determinants, what is \( \det(AB) \)?
solution: For square matrices, \( \det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B) \). Here that is \( ([[detA]])\cdot([[detB]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two determinants instead of multiplying them.
This computes \( \det(A+B) \) instead of \( \det(AB) \); addition and multiplication of determinants follow different rules.
This is just \( \det(A) \), ignoring \( B \) entirely.
This is just \( \det(B) \), ignoring \( A \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{bmatrix} \), so that \( \det(A) = [[detA]] \). Since \( A \) is invertible, what is \( \det(A^{-1}) \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the reciprocal and just gives \( \det(A) \) back.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This negates \( \det(A) \) instead of taking its reciprocal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
